son-in-law’s protection, would have been under wretched alarm every night of his life  The strength, resolution, and presence of mind of the Mr  Knightleys, commanded his fullest dependence  While either of them protected him and his, Hartfield was safe —But Mr  John Knightley must be in London again by the end of the first week in November The result of this distress was, that, with a much more voluntary, cheerful consent than his daughter had ever presumed to hope for at the moment, she was able to fix her wedding-day—and Mr  Elton was called on, within a month from the marriage of Mr  and Mrs  Robert Martin, to join the hands of Mr  Knightley and Miss Woodhouse The wedding was very much like other weddings, where the parties have no taste for finery or parade; and Mrs  Elton, from the particulars detailed by her husband, thought it all extremely shabby, and very inferior to her own —“Very little white satin, very few lace veils; a most pitiful business —Selina would stare when she heard of it ”—But, in spite of these deficiencies, the wishes, the hopes, the confidence, the predictions of the small band of true friends who witnessed the ceremony, were fully answered in the perfect happiness of the union FINIS             *** END OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK EMMA *** Updated editions will replace the previous one—the old editions will be renamed Creating the works from print editions not protected by U S  copyright law means that no one owns a United States copyright in these works, so the Foundation (and you ) can copy and distribute it in the United States without permission and without paying copyright royalties  Special rules, set forth in the General Terms of Use part of this license, apply to copying and distributing Project Gutenberg™ electronic works to protect the PROJECT GUTENBERG™ concept and trademark  Project Gutenberg is a registered trademark, and may not be used if you charge for an eBook, except by following the terms of the trademark license, including paying royalties for use of the Project Gutenberg trademark  If you do not charge anything for copies of this eBook, complying with the trademark license is very easy  You may use this eBook for nearly any purpose such as creation of derivative works, reports, performances and research  Project Gutenberg eBooks may be modified and printed and given away—you may do practically ANYTHING in the United States with eBooks not protected by U

The text is a conversation between Mr. Weston and Mrs. Elton, discussing the impending move of the Churchill family from Enscombe in Yorkshire to London due to Mrs. Churchill’s poor health, though Mr. Weston suspects her illness may be exaggerated and that she simply desires a change of scenery. Mrs. Elton defends women’s sensibilities, particularly regarding travel and comfort, and shares her sister Selina’s aversion to staying in inns. She also expresses disdain for social upstarts, drawing a parallel to the Tupmans, a newly wealthy family in her neighborhood who behave as if they are equals to established families despite their humble origins. Mr. Weston reveals his skepticism about Mrs. Churchill’s health and her pretensions, calling her an arrogant

Emma Woodhouse, though initially reluctant, is encouraged by Mrs. Elton to visit Bath, which Mrs. Elton praises for its health benefits and vibrant social life. Emma politely declines, citing her father’s health and his past unsuccessful attempts at Bath, as well as her own preference for staying close to home. Mrs. Elton, eager to promote her own social influence, insists on introducing Emma to the best society in Bath, offering connections through her friend Mrs. Partridge and proposing a musical club. Emma is deeply offended by the implication of needing such an introduction, feeling it undermines her dignity. Despite this, she remains composed. Later, Mrs. Elton discusses plans for a casual garden party, suggesting a rustic, informal outing with baskets

Emma is deeply irritated by Mrs. Elton’s pretentiousness, vanity, and lack of genuine manners, especially her condescending remarks about Mrs. Weston and her flippant familiarity with Mr. Knightley. She finds Mrs. Elton’s behavior insufferable—her exaggerated compliments, self-importance, and false claims of refinement. Emma is particularly offended by Mrs. Elton’s assumption of intimacy and her condescending attitude toward those she deems beneath her, including her own governess, Mrs. Weston. She contrasts Mrs. Elton’s vulgarity with the genuine grace of others, like Mr. Knightley and Harriet, and is delighted when Knightley dances with Harriet, a moment of pure joy for her.

The text describes a conversation among several characters, primarily focusing on Mrs. Elton’s enthusiasm for organizing a visit to Donwell, which she insists on managing herself. She plans to bring Jane Fairfax and her aunt, and while Mr. Knightley politely declines her offer to invite guests, he firmly states that only Mrs. Knightley can have such authority. Mrs. Elton is amused and satisfied by his remark, interpreting it as a compliment. The discussion shifts to Mr. Elton’s failed attempt to meet Mr. Knightley at Donwell, where he was unexpectedly absent despite a prior arrangement. Mrs. Elton expresses indignation on his behalf, blaming the servants, while Mr. Elton laments the wasted, hot walk. Emma Wood

Emma Woodhouse observes Mrs. Elton's growing coldness and ill will toward her and Harriet Smith, marked by sneering and neglectful behavior. Despite this, Mrs. Elton lavishes excessive attention on Jane Fairfax, whom Emma finds puzzling—especially since Jane tolerates Mrs. Elton’s company despite her obvious pretensions. Emma suspects Jane is under some hidden obligation or penance, refusing generous invitations from the Dixons to remain in Highbury. Mrs. Weston suggests Jane may be seeking relief from her aunt’s company, while Mr. Knightley adds that Mrs. Elton, though insincere in public, treats Jane with quiet respect in person—acknowledging her superiority in mind and manner. Emma, initially

Mrs. Elton, newly arrived and full of enthusiasm, is captivated by Hartfield, which she finds strikingly similar to her beloved Maple Grove, her brother Mr. Suckling’s estate. She praises the house, grounds, and staircase, comparing them to Maple Grove and expressing deep affection for the place, which she considers a second home. She plans to explore the surrounding countryside with her brother and sister, who are expected in the spring, and intends to use their barouche-landau for outings. Mrs. Elton is determined to help Jane Fairfax find a suitable governess position, insisting on a prestigious, elegant household like those at Maple Grove or Mrs. Bragge’s, despite Jane’s reluctance and preference for independence

Emma Woodhouse, confident in her matchmaking abilities, has long planned for Harriet Smith to marry Mr. Elton, believing it a suitable and natural match. She is pleased with her own foresight, having conceived the idea the very first evening Harriet arrived at Hartfield. Mr. Elton, a respectable, well-meaning man with a comfortable home and independent income, is seen by Emma as an ideal match for Harriet, especially since Harriet is clearly smitten with him. Emma is also beginning to consider Frank Churchill’s potential affection for Harriet, seeing it as a possible consolation if Frank’s feelings for her are unreciprocated. However, her plans are disrupted when Frank Churchill unexpectedly arrives at Hartfield with Harriet

Mrs. Weston is deeply content with her marriage to Mr. Weston, enjoying the comfort of Randalls and the ease of proximity to Hartfield, which allows frequent visits. Emma, though initially concerned about her father’s sorrow over Miss Taylor’s marriage, is reassured by Mrs. Weston’s happiness and the practical benefits of the arrangement. Mr. Frank Churchill’s absence disappoints Mrs. Weston, but Mr. Weston finds comfort in the hope of a later visit, especially after learning Frank’s aunt has moved to Richmond, making visits more feasible. The prospect of a ball at the Crown, now confirmed, brings joy, and Emma, despite initial reluctance, agrees to attend. The ball proceeds smoothly, and Emma and Frank meet with mutual anticipation. Meanwhile

Emma reflects deeply on her misguided interference in Harriet Smith’s life, realizing her errors have caused great harm. She acknowledges that her belief in Mr. Elton’s affection for Harriet was entirely mistaken, fueled by her own vanity and misjudgment. She recognizes that Mr. Knightley, whom she has long admired, has always been the true superior in her heart, and that her fleeting interest in Frank Churchill was never real.
